title: Zea perennis
intro: Zea perennis, also known as perennial teosinte, is a wild grass native to Central America, belonging to the same genus as corn and containing the highest concentration of zeaxanthin, a carotenoid pigment. The health benefits of Zea perennis include its ability to protect the eyes from age-related macular degeneration and improve vision in people with glaucoma, as well as its potential to reduce inflammation and improve cardiovascular health due to its high antioxidant content. The bioactive constituents of Zea perennis include zeaxanthin, lutein, and other carotenoids, as well as phenolic acids and flavonoids. Zea perennis is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as capsules, tablets, and eye drops, which are used to support eye health and vision.
taxonomy: Zea perennis is a perennial wild grass species within the genus Zea, which belongs to the family Poaceae. It is a member of the subfamily Panicoideae and the tribe Maydeae. The classification of Zea perennis is further divided into the subgenus Zea and the section Zea. The species Zea perennis is closely related to Zea mays, the domesticated corn. The taxonomic classification of Zea perennis is a subject of ongoing research and may be subject to change.

parts: Zea perennis is a perennial wild grass native to North America, and it has been used in traditional medicine by various Native American tribes. The plant's roots, leaves, and seeds have been employed in treatments for various ailments. Its medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of saponins, flavonoids, and other bioactive compounds.
